title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Origin (Ethiopia → Yemen)
description: Kaffa highlands in Ethiopia where coffee evolved naturally in forest understory. Layer 1 (historical trade) = brown.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Origin (Ethiopia → Yemen)
description: Ethiopian highlands en route to the Red Sea coast.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Origin (Ethiopia → Yemen)
description: Bab-el-Mandeb — the Red Sea strait coffee crossed to reach Arabia.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Origin (Ethiopia → Yemen)
description: Mocha (Al-Mukha) Yemen — Sufi monks used coffee for night prayer by the 15th century.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Ottoman Spread (Yemen → Vienna)
description: Mocha — entry into the Ottoman trade network.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Ottoman Spread (Yemen → Vienna)
description: Mecca — early ban on coffee lifted as trade value became clear.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Ottoman Spread (Yemen → Vienna)
description: Cairo — major Ottoman coffee trading hub.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Ottoman Spread (Yemen → Vienna)
description: Damascus — Silk Road junction.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Ottoman Spread (Yemen → Vienna)
description: Constantinople (Istanbul) — first coffeehouses opened 1550s as 'schools of the wise'.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Ottoman Spread (Yemen → Vienna)
description: Vienna — Ottoman coffee reached Europe after the 1683 siege.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: European Coffeehouses
description: Istanbul — the coffeehouse model spreads west.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: European Coffeehouses
description: Venice — Europe's first commercial coffee imports (early 1600s).

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: European Coffeehouses
description: Paris — Café Procope (1686) becomes a center of Enlightenment debate.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: European Coffeehouses
description: Amsterdam — Dutch East India Company coffee re-exports.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: European Coffeehouses
description: London — 'Penny Universities' and the birth of Lloyd's insurance + the Stock Exchange.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Dutch Colonial Transplant (to Java)
description: Mocha — Dutch ships smuggle coffee seedlings out of Yemen around 1616.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Dutch Colonial Transplant (to Java)
description: Cape of Good Hope — maritime route around Africa.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Dutch Colonial Transplant (to Java)
description: Batavia (Jakarta) Java — Dutch force cultivation; the Cultuurstelsel system uses coerced labor.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Dutch Colonial Transplant (to Java)
description: Amsterdam — coffee returns as a Dutch monopoly commodity.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: French Colonial to Brazil
description: Paris — King Louis XIV's 1714 gift seedling becomes the ancestor of New World coffee.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: French Colonial to Brazil
description: Martinique — Gabriel de Clieu plants the seedling in 1720 using enslaved labor.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: French Colonial to Brazil
description: Saint-Domingue (Haiti) — by 1788 produces half the world's coffee on enslaved labor.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: French Colonial to Brazil
description: Rio de Janeiro — coffee enters Brazil in 1727 via Pará.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: French Colonial to Brazil
description: Santos — São Paulo coffee boom makes Brazil the world's largest producer by the 1830s (on enslaved labor until abolition in 1888).

title: LAYER 2 — Language Diffusion: the word 'coffee' traces its trade route
description: Ethiopia — 'buna' (Oromo/Amharic). Layer 2 (language) = purple. Each linguistic transformation marks a cultural and economic exchange.

title: LAYER 2 — Language Diffusion: the word 'coffee' traces its trade route
description: Yemen — 'qahwa' (Arabic). Originally meant 'wine'; repurposed for this new stimulating drink.

title: LAYER 2 — Language Diffusion: the word 'coffee' traces its trade route
description: Turkey — 'kahve' (Turkish). Ottoman adaptation, spread via imperial trade.

title: LAYER 2 — Language Diffusion: the word 'coffee' traces its trade route
description: Italy — 'caffè' (Italian). Venetian merchants Italianize the Ottoman word.

title: LAYER 2 — Language Diffusion: the word 'coffee' traces its trade route
description: France — 'café' (French). Later adopted by Spanish and Portuguese.

title: LAYER 2 — Language Diffusion: the word 'coffee' traces its trade route
description: England — 'coffee' (English). The anglicized spelling enters the global trade lexicon.

title: LAYER 3 — Modern Supply Chain (today)
description: Santos Brazil — 38% of global production ships from here. Layer 3 (modern) = gray.

title: LAYER 3 — Modern Supply Chain (today)
description: Ho Chi Minh City Vietnam — Central Highlands Robusta is 17.7% of global supply.

title: LAYER 3 — Modern Supply Chain (today)
description: Addis Ababa Ethiopia — specialty Arabica origin market.

title: LAYER 3 — Modern Supply Chain (today)
description: Buenaventura Colombia — 100% Arabica port of departure.

title: LAYER 3 — Modern Supply Chain (today)
description: Rotterdam — Europe's largest coffee import terminal.

title: LAYER 3 — Modern Supply Chain (today)
description: New York — ICE Futures US sets the global Arabica price via the C-market.
